rulings.Case Title: JOHN H. DANNER III et al VS. THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: (Redacted) Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Compel The Personal Contact Information Of The 2008 H-20 Examination Raters, Percipient Witnesses, Req. In The Special Interrogatories, Set One, Of Raycardo Aviles; Statement
rulings.Rulings: Hearing Set for Thursday, June 27, 2013, Line 1: Plaintiffs JOHN DANNER, DONALD DURKEE, RICHARD COTTON, and RAYCARDO AVILES' (Redacted) Motion to Compel The Personal Contact Information Of The 2008 H-20 Examination Raters, Percipient Witnesses, Request In The Special Interrogatories, Set One, of Raycardo Aviles; Statement Pro Tem Judge Adrian Colley, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. No Tentative Ruling, except that plaintiffs' motion for sanctions is denied. Among the matters to be addressed by counsel the following should be addressed: 1. Plaintiff's papers indicate that Judge Harold Kahn in late 2011 orally ordered that defendants did not have "to disclose the contact information of the Raters", but with the "caveat" that "Should plaintiffs be able to articulate a more specific need for the Rater contact information in the future.." "reconsideration " would be possible "based on ... these new facts". If so, what new facts do plaintiffs advance here to warrant any such reconsideration? 2. By contrast, defendants' papers say that in January, 2012, "the Court" addressed the issue and "did not order disclosure of raters' personal contact information". Please reconcile the parties' somewhat differing contentions here. 3. The Raters have signed confidentiality agreements. Even if Rater personal contact information were available how could the Raters provide information? 4. Why does not the Rater business contact information suffice particularly in view of the fact that it has already been availed of by plaintiffs? 5. Should not the personal risks to the Raters in the disclosure of personal contact information be an important factor, particularily in light of recent well publicized incidents involving murders of law enforcement officers at home, and of which the Court should take judicial notice? 6. Please address the Planned Parenthood and Puerto decisions as they may apply here. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: SAEED J IBRAHIM VS. EXTREME AUTO RECOVERY COMPANY
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Compel Discovery And For Discovery Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Hearing Set for Thursday, June 27, 2013, Line 2: Plaintiff SAEED IBRAHIM'S Motion to Compel Discovery and For Discovery Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Adrian Colley, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Pro per plaintiff's motion to compel discovery and for discovery sanctions is denied. As per the request of defendant Jones' counsel made in their papers, plaintiff is ordered to meet and confer with such counsel regarding the proper means for obtaining the deposition testimony of defendant Jones while he is incarcerated in the State of Illinois. Plaintiff apparently seeks to discover defendant Jones' "resident address" and to compel his attendance at a deposition. Defendant Jones is incarcerated in an Illinois correctional institution, the details of which plaintiff is fully aware. Plaintiff has also been advised of the difficulties of taking such a deposition, something that counsel for defendant Jones are willing to agree to so long as the necessary statutory procedures are followed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to adrianacolley@aol.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: BONNIE LAU VS. DELOITTE TAX LLP, A LIMITED LIABILITY PARTNERSHIP et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Quash Subpoenas Of Employment Records
rulings.Rulings: Hearing Set for Thursday, June 27, 2013, Line 5: Plaintiff BONNIE LAU'S Motion to Quash Subpoenas of Employment Records Pro Tem Judge Adrian Colley, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's motion to quash defendant Deloitte Tax's ("defendant") subpoenas for plaintiff's employment records is denied. Plaintiff's position with defendant was eliminated. In plaintiff's ensuing lawsuit Defendant subpoened plaintiff's personnel records from four previous employers, which subpoenas plaintiff moved to quash. Plaintiff does not appear to contest the fact that such subpoenas to plaintiffs in employment disputes for their personnel records from previous employers are not uncommon. Defendant centers its legal argument around Sirota v. Penske Truck Leasing Corp., No. C05-03296 SI, 2006 WL 708910, at * 2(N.D. Cal. March 17, 2006), (and similar cases) which specifically allowed a defendant employer to discover the plaintiff's personnel records from prior employers. In addition, plaintiff's employment application to defendant authorized defendant to "make an independent investigation" of plaintiff's past employment. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to adrianacolley@aol.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MARK EMMETT WILSON VS. MIGUEL ALEJANDRO RESCALVO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Production Of Documents Requested Under Demand For Production Of Documents, Set One No. 44; Request For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Hearing Set for Thursday, June 27, 2013, Line 6: Plaintiff MARK WILSON'S Motion to Compel Production of Documents Requested Under Demand for Production of Documents, Set One No. 44; Request for Monetary Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Adrian Colley, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Pro per plaintiff's motion to compel production of documents pursuant to a demand for production of documents, set one, no. 44, ("no. 44"), and request for sanctions, is 1) partially granted to the extent of requiring defendant Det Norsk Veritas (U.S.A.) Inc. ("Det Norsk") to produce the document requested in no. 44, " a copy of the internal audit conducted at One Bush in December of 2011 by Luc Larmuseau", in a redacted format so that the language therein with respect to praising plaintiff's work at Det Norsk, suggesting that it be "a model", and praising plaintiff's "energy", and for having "secured projects", as summarized in plaintiff's moving papers, remains in the redacted document; and 2) denied insofar as it seeks monetary sanctions against defendants. This ruling will provide plaintiff with what he appears to believe is important to his case in what may be in the internal audit report, while at the same time protecting other information in the report from discovery by plaintiff that does not appear to be relevant to his case, and possibly covered by the attorney client privilege, although it is not clear how the privilege might be present with respect to the report. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to adrianacolley@aol.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CONNIE H. SEBASTIAN VS. SURETY LIFE INSURANCE COMPANY
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, June 26, 2013, Line 17, DEFENDANT SURETY LIFE INSURANCE COMPANY DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. The demurrer to the first cause of action for breach of contract is overruled. The statute of frauds (CC §1624(a)(1)) bars an oral contract that "by its terms is not to be performed within a year of the making thereof." The allegations of the cross complaint do not conclusively show that the contract runs afoul of the statute of frauds. There is a factual issue regarding the precise terms of the agreement. Even if the contract was for personal services and terminated upon death, paragraph 13 alleges that the parties "reiterated" the contract and paragraph 27 alleges that they "verbally affirmed" the contract. Accordingly, the parties ratified the prior contract and/or entered into a new agreement. The statute of limitations argument fails because paragraph 17 alleges that the breach occurred within the last two years prior to the filing of the action. The demurrer to the second cause of action for conversion is overruled. Conversion and fraud are distinct causes of action and conversion does not need to be pleaded with the same particularity as an intentional misrepresentation claim. There is no statute of limitations issue because paragraph 33 alleges that the conversion was discovered within the past 3 years prior to the filing of the action. The Court overrules the demurrer to the third cause of action. Nothing in the cross-complaint shows that the claim is time barred. It is unclear when Ms. Chin made the expenditures and paragraph 17 indicates that there is no statute of limitations issue. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Rulings: Hearing Set for Thursday, June 27, 2013, Line 8: Defendant PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC COMPANY'S Motion to Compel Plaintiffs Deposition for Sanctions and for a Protective Order Pro Tem Judge Adrian Colley, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion of defendant P.G.& E. to compel plaintiff's deposition, for a protective order, and for sanctions to the extent of $750 is granted. Defendant has made considerable efforts though a meet and confer and otherwise to schedule the deposition of plaintiff. Plaintiff has been dilatory, somewhat non-responsive, and prevaricating in return to the point that to date defendant has not been able to schedule its requested deposition. Defendant P.G.&E.'s interrogatories are relevant, not onerous, and should be timely responded to by plaintiff. The motion's request for sanctions has been reduced in amount to the amount specified above. rulings.Case Title: JOHANNA PACE VS. TRUCK INSURANCE EXCHANGE
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Request For Admission, Set One
rulings.Rulings: Hearing Set for Thursday, June 27, 2013, Line 7: Plaintiff JOHANNA PACE'S Motion to Compel Further Responses to Request for Admission, Set One Pro Tem Judge Adrian Colley, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Claimant's motions to compel further responses to requests for admission, set one, and to form interrogatories, set two, no.17.1, as well as for sanctions, are denied. At issue are four requests for admission, and form interrogatory no. 17.1, which requires respondent to state, with respect to each corresponding request for admission that was denied, the facts, witnesses and documents upon which the denial was based. Of the four requests, both parties appear to concede that there are defects in request no. 1 obviating respondent's need to respond thereto. The overly broad request no. 2 requests an admission with respect to the "sole cause of the subject accident". Under the complicated and yet to be fully ascertained circumstances of the accident, where the principal actors have invoked their constitutional rights, and where the requested admissions relate to third party conduct outside of respondent's presence, respondent's denial of request no. 2 was proper, as was its refusal for similar reasons to admit request no. 3 as to whether a vehicle in question was "an uninsured motor vehicle". Request no. 4 was also properly denied for the reasons cited by respondent. With respect to form interrogatory 17.1, under the circumstances, respondent has properly responded to that, given the issues with respect to the requests. Sanctions in favor of claimant are not warranted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to adrianacolley@aol.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
